items.title: Why river management is so crucial to protecting one of world's most important wetlands
items.summary: A major new study led by Swansea University has shown that upstream river management, rather than local rainfall, is the primary driver of one of the world's most important wetlands. According to the research published in Scientific Reports, which analyzed more than two decades of satellite obs

items.title: Creating the largest-ever database on floating ocean plastic using smart cameras
items.summary: The first five years (2019–2024) of operations of The Ocean Cleanup's Automated Debris Imaging System (ADIS) have been reviewed, and the findings have been published in Environmental Research Communications. In addition to reviewing the technology's performance, the study covers the initia

items.title: CRISPR-Cas coordinates a layered bacterial defense network in innate immunity
items.summary: All cellular life—from humans to bacteria—possesses two layers of immunity: innate and adaptive. Scientists have long understood how these two types of immunity work together in higher organisms but not in the microbial world.

items.title: Q&A: Listening with light—an innovative new technology for studying the seafloor
items.summary: The seafloor plays an important ecological and societal role. Understanding the processes that shape this environment will help guide conservation policy and decision-making about underwater infrastructure and geohazards.

items.title: Nova cjepiva protiv kuge pokazala 100% učinkovitost na miševima
items.summary: DVA NOVA cjepiva pokazala su 100% učinkovitost protiv kuge na miševima, uključujući smrtonosni plućni oblik. To budi nadu za prvu odobrenu zaštitu, posebno zbog rastuće otpornosti na antibiotike.

items.title: First ever reported cryo-EM visualization of E. coli TGT structure
items.summary: The enzyme tRNA-guanine transglycosylase (TGT) chemically modifies transfer RNA (tRNA) and plays a key role in how disease-causing bacteria control the production of proteins needed to cause infection. E. coli TGT is nearly identical to the one found in Shigella, which causes the intestinal infectio

items.title: Are penalties in the US airline industry a deterrent or a 'price?'
items.summary: Many people drive more carefully for a while after receiving a traffic fine. But do large corporations, such as airlines, also change their behavior when they face financial penalties? We examine this question by studying penalties imposed in the United States for lengthy tarmac delays.

items.title: New optical method follows single proteins as they shift shape
items.summary: Researchers at the University of Twente have developed an optical method that follows the shape changes of a single protein in liquid without attaching anything to it. It reads the protein's structure from its own molecular vibrations, free from the labels or tags that other techniques rely on.

items.title: Physics-based AI could boost biomedical imaging and autonomous vehicle sensors
items.summary: A research team led by UCLA and the University of Rochester has demonstrated a promising evolution of an imaging system designed to capture details within "complex media," which scatter light, from depicting structures inside body tissue to seeing obstacles through heavy fog. The system uses physics

items.title: Molecular model for semiconductor surfaces developed
items.summary: Semiconductor surfaces can generally be studied only with considerable experimental effort, for example, in an ultrahigh vacuum. To more easily gain new insights into their properties and the possibilities for targeted modification, scientists at Heidelberg University have developed a molecular mode

items.title: More connections can deepen polarization when social ties remain weak
items.summary: Social networks, intended to bring people together, can actually increase polarization, new research finds, because while the links in the network may be plentiful, they're probably weak. A research team including Cornell sociologist Michael Macy has found that while the growing number of inter

items.title: NASA to Showcase Agency’s Newest Wind Tunnel in Virginia
items.summary: Media are invited to NASA’s Langley Research Center in Hampton, Virginia, on Friday, July 31, to attend a media tour and ribbon-cutting ceremony for the Flight Dynamics Research Facility, the agency’s first new wind tunnel in more than 40 years. The event will include a brief media availability with

items.title: Chemists develop a molecular platform for the selective control of oxygen reaction pathways
items.summary: Controlling how oxygen reacts is important for improving technologies such as batteries, fuel cells and environmentally sustainable chemical processes. A research team led by professor Seung Jun Hwang from KAIST's Department of Chemistry has developed a molecular system capable of directing oxy

items.title: France recorded 5,700 more deaths than usual during its historic June heat wave
items.summary: France recorded at least 5,700 more deaths than usual during a historic heat wave last month, the public health agency said Wednesday, sharply increasing its estimates of the feared toll from record-shattering temperatures.

items.title: Dating apps like Tinder affect relationships and the economics of desire
items.summary: In recent years, it has become commonplace to see people selecting potential partners with the same gestures (and enthusiasm) they use to scroll through videos on TikTok or browse products in an online store. Swipe right for interest; swipe left to move on. What once involved the drama of meeting so

items.title: Spread of invasive malaria mosquito from Asia to Africa traced in new genomic study
items.summary: A genomic surveillance study has shed new light on how an invasive urban malaria mosquito species spread from Asia to Africa, potentially threatening public health across a rapidly urbanizing continent where malaria is mainly concentrated in rural areas.

items.title: Robotic Servicing Mission Launches with NASA Support
items.summary: Following its liftoff from Cape Canaveral on July 21 aboard a SpaceX Falcon 9 rocket, the Mission Robotic Vehicle (MRV) hosting the NASA-supported Robotic Servicing of Geosynchronous Satellites (RSGS) payload is now en route to geosynchronous Earth orbit, where it will use its advanced robotics to s

items.title: Antibiotic-tolerant bacteria rely on a hidden RNA-based survival strategy
items.summary: Antibiotics are a medical marvel, but many microbes are gaining the ability to resist and tolerate these therapeutics. St. Jude Children's Research Hospital scientists have uncovered how S. pneumoniae adapts to antibiotic exposure and immune pressures. Their findings reveal that changes in RNA

items.title: New genetic approaches raise hopes for restoring the American chestnut to its former glory
items.summary: The American chestnut tree was a dominant and iconic species in many Appalachian forests until it was devastated by an introduced fungus from Asia called chestnut blight.

items.title: Zagađenje zraka povećava rizik od razvoja Parkinsonove bolesti
items.summary: NOVA meta-analiza potvrđuje da dugotrajna izloženost zagađenom zraku, posebno česticama PM2.5 i PM10, statistički povećava rizik od razvoja Parkinsonove bolesti.

items.title: Carbon footprint of metal production could be more than 10 times higher than estimated
items.summary: New research from the University of St Andrews has found that metal production carbon footprints could be more than 10 times higher than previously estimated, with serious implications for the ambition to reach net zero.

items.title: Rescuing satellites is harder than it looks. A growing space junk problem will make doing so more important
items.summary: A commercial robotic spacecraft called LINK launched on an emergency mission to save NASA's Neil Gehrels Swift Observatory, which has been slowly falling toward Earth, on July 3, 2026. Over the coming weeks, LINK, built by Katalyst Space Technologies, will try to grasp the telescope, then spend

items.title: Storms that built a graveyard: Ancient monsoons created a spectacular dinosaur fossil site
items.summary: Around 80 million years ago, a stretch of tropical coastline in what is now northeastern Italy was lashed by monsoon rains and tropical cyclones. Those storms swept dinosaur carcasses, crocodiles, plants and river mud onto a tidal flat, where the tides and a film of microbes sealed everything away w

items.title: Soft plastic recycling laws have been delayed. An expert explains why
items.summary: Mandatory curbside collection of soft plastic (chip bags, bread bags, plastic wrap) has just been pushed back from 2027 to 2030.

items.title: Greece broils on year's warmest day as three-day heat wave peaks
items.summary: Greece is expected to experience its hottest day of the year Wednesday, with temperatures reaching up to 43°C (109°F), according to meteorologists. Several regions have been placed on alert because of the risk of wildfires.

items.title: NASA Sets Coverage for Astronaut Chris Williams, Crewmates Return
items.summary: NASA astronaut Chris Williams and Roscosmos cosmonauts Sergey Kud-Sverchkov and Sergei Mikaev are wrapping up their 241‑day mission aboard the International Space Station. The crew and its Soyuz MS-28 spacecraft will undock from the orbiting laboratory’s Rassvet module at 3:02 a.m. EDT Sunday, July

items.title: EU watchdog reduces safe level of exposure to 'forever chemical' TFA
items.summary: The EU's food safety agency said Wednesday it has dramatically reduced the acceptable daily intake level of the so-called forever chemical TFA, following new evidence of its effect on a key hormone that regulates the body's metabolism.

items.title: Juno takes temperature of Jupiter's fiery moon Io
items.summary: NASA's Juno mission has provided the first measurements of the temperature below the surface of Jupiter's moon Io, revealing significant heating within the shallow subsurface of the most volcanically active world in the solar system. Collected during two close flybys, the data also show th

items.title: The violence link: Animal maltreatment and family violence often happen together
items.summary: For some survivors of intimate partner violence, leaving an abusive relationship might mean facing a difficult choice between prioritizing their own safety and that of a beloved pet.

items.title: Solar-powered process turns CO₂ into protein-building amino acids
items.summary: According to United Nations projections, global food demand could increase by around 60% by 2050, while only about 2% of additional agricultural land is expected to become available. Researchers at the Technical University of Munich (TUM) are therefore exploring new approaches to safeguard food secu

items.title: A New Look – and Sound – for Messier 94
items.summary: NASA’s Chandra X-ray Observatory unveiled this new look at the galaxy NGC 4736, also known as Messier 94, on June 30, 2026. Messier 94 is a spiral galaxy with a bright inner ring around it, called a starburst ring, where new stars are forming, perhaps fueled by gas driven in the unique oval-sh

items.title: Engineered enzyme erases a stubborn mark of aging by up to 70% in human tissue samples
items.summary: A biotech company called Revel Pharmaceuticals is looking into ways to reverse aging, and the company's science team, along with researchers from the company Calico and the University of Colorado, may be a step closer to realizing the so-called fountain of youth. The team recently published the

items.title: Researchers develop new AI system for highly accurate mapping and modeling of orchards
items.summary: In precision agriculture, where data is used to automate and optimize agricultural tasks for maximum yield, large-scale mapping and digital modeling of the land of interest are essential. This digital backbone enables tasks such as yield estimation, targeted spraying and phenotyping while also provi

items.title: Živimo dulje, ali ne i zdravije, pokazuje nova studija
items.summary: GLOBALNA studija otkriva da ljudi žive dulje, ali sve veći dio života provode narušenog zdravlja. Stručnjaci pozivaju na promjenu fokusa sa same dugovječnosti na poboljšanje kvalitete starenja.

items.title: How 'super' enzymes can transform recycling
items.summary: From microplastics clogging up our oceans to landfills leaching dangerous chemicals, plastic waste is a serious and growing problem for human health and the environment. We need to recycle more and, crucially, recycle better.

items.title: New study links mothers' stronger ethnic-racial identity to higher social skills in young children
items.summary: As children head back to school this fall, one of the most important lessons won't be found in a textbook. It will happen as they learn to share, cooperate and build friendships. New University of Michigan research suggests that a mother's stronger connection to her ethnic-racial identity

items.title: Microscale roughness breakthrough defies 80 years of fluid dynamics
items.summary: Logically, you would think a sleek surface has optimal aerodynamics—but recent research at Tohoku University turns this fundamental principle on its head. Applying an irregular microscale surface texture reduced the aerodynamic drag of a test model. The innovation has potential applications in the d

items.title: There are gaps in food access in Georgia, but not where you'd expect
items.summary: In 2023, more than 50 million people across the United States turned to food pantries and other charitable organizations for meals. But many may be unable to access the food they need, particularly if they live in a more well-off area, according to a new study from the University of Georgia publishe

items.title: Ordinary moss generates surprisingly complex electrical activity
items.summary: Moss may well be something that most of us barely give a second thought, but there is far more to this small nonvascular plant than meets the eye. A new study published in the journal Royal Society Open Science reports that it displays a surprising amount of electrical activity.

items.title: Combined nanoparticle and internal laser method could make cancer therapy less invasive
items.summary: In a promising development for cancer treatment, a collaborative research team has overcome two issues that have prevented photothermal therapy, a much less invasive treatment option than surgery and/or radiation, from becoming more common.

items.title: Great Barrier Reef microbiome map reveals more than 500 new bacterial species
items.summary: In 2012, Nature revealed that humans provide a home to thousands of different species of microbes in a series of papers that opened a path to understanding the impact of the human microbiome on our health.

items.title: Global analysis reveals age as overlooked driver of food-related climate emissions
items.summary: A new international study published in Nature Food reveals that age plays an important but often overlooked role in shaping global food-related greenhouse gas emissions. The study shows that dietary emissions can vary substantially across people's life stages, reflecting differences in nutritio

items.title: Fruit flies turn fleeting scent puffs into directional memories, study finds
items.summary: When a fruit fly catches a whiff of a ripe peach, it quickly heads toward it. Time after time, it finds the fruit—even from many feet away and even if the scent arrives only as a few drifting, disconnected puffs. How do they home in with such unerring accuracy?

items.title: Transmissible cancer cells discovered in fish act more like parasites than traditional tumors
items.summary: A team of Vermont researchers has discovered that mysterious black skin lesions afflicting catfish in Lake Memphremagog and other New England and Canadian lakes are caused by a transmissible cancer—the first identified in a freshwater fish species. The cancer cells behave more like parasites than co

items.title: Mapping Io’s Hidden Heat With NASA’s Juno
items.summary: Description This graphic illustrates the areas of Jupiter’s moon Io sampled by the Microwave Radiometer (MWR) instrument aboard NASA’s Juno spacecraft during two close flybys. The black overlapping lines show the instrument’s footprints during Perijove 57 on Dec. 30, 2023, when the spacecraft primar

items.title: Jupiter-mass 'exomoon' orbiting brown dwarf challenges cosmic labels
items.summary: Observations made with the European Southern Observatory's Very Large Telescope (ESO's VLT) have revealed evidence for a moon-like object in the CD-35 2722 system. Unlike moons in our solar system, the newly found object does not orbit a planet, raising questions about what to name it. Ins

items.title: Genetically distinct avocados discovered in Nicaragua
items.summary: A team of scientists led by researchers at the Smithsonian's National Museum of Natural History and Texas A&M University recently uncovered genetic traces of ancient migration in DNA from avocados growing in Nicaragua. They found evidence that, around 5,000 years ago, people migrating north fro

items.title: NASA’s Juno Peers Beneath Io’s Surface
items.summary: Description This map represents data captured by the Microwave Radiometer (MWR) aboard NASA’s Juno spacecraft, indicating heat rising from just beneath the surface of Jupiter’s moon Io. While infrared instruments measure the temperature of the moon’s surface, the lowest frequency microwave channels

items.title: NASA’s Juno Takes Temperature of Jupiter’s Fiery Moon Io
items.summary: Lee esta historia en español aquí.  NASA’s Juno mission has provided the first measurements of the temperature below the surface of Jupiter’s moon Io, revealing significant heating within the shallow subsurface of the most volcanically active world in the solar system. Collected during two clos

items.title: Different lessons, shared kindness: Global study finds children's social behavior becomes more alike with age
items.summary: Children in one country may be encouraged to share their toys, while those in another are taught to show kindness through empathy or building strong social bonds. Despite these different approaches, a new study led by the University of Lincoln has found that children from different cultures become m

items.title: Green LED light could help keep leafy greens fresher longer, study suggests
items.summary: Low-intensity green lighting in refrigerated storage can slow spoilage in leafy greens, helping maintain their appearance and nutritional value, according to a new study from McGill University. The research, published in the Journal of Food Science, tested how different lighting conditions affect fr

items.title: Removing 'forever chemicals' from farmlands—at a fraction of the cost
items.summary: A Yale-led research team has proposed a less expensive—and more environmentally friendly—method for removing certain "forever chemicals" from millions of acres of farmland in the United States and around the world.

items.title: Astronomers are still trying to understand how a galaxy's spiral arms form
items.summary: The Whirlpool galaxy (M51) is a classic grand spiral galaxy. It was the first object identified as a spiral, way back in 1845. At the time, it and other galaxies were considered spiral nebulae, since astronomers didn't know other galaxies existed. Edwin Hubble finally proved that there were oth

items.title: NASA Joins Genesis Mission to Accelerate AI-Driven Discovery
items.summary: NASA is supporting the Genesis Mission, a national effort to drive the use of artificial intelligence in tackling complex scientific and engineering challenges to advance a new era of discovery. President Donald J. Trump issued the Executive Order “Launching the Genesis Mission” on Nov. 24, 2025, cr

items.title: Scientists warn of potential global marine invasive species event linked to idle ships in the Strait of Hormuz
items.summary: New research from an international team of 24 marine scientists, led by the University of Maryland Center for Environmental Science (UMCES) and including the Woods Hole Oceanographic Institution (WHOI), cautions that thousands of commercial vessels stranded for months in the Persian/Arabian Gulf fol

items.title: Black coral shapes influence how microscopic cilia move food and oxygen
items.summary: An international research team led by scientists from the Max Planck Institute for Marine Microbiology in Bremen and the Leibniz Institute for Baltic Sea Research Warnemünde (IOW) investigated how the shape of black corals influences the functions of their cilia. The findings are published in the jo

items.title: Left or right? Moths use a preferred side to inspect flowers for nectar
items.summary: Just as humans exhibit right- or left-handedness, moths also have a preferred side on which they place their proboscis when inspecting a flower for nectar. This observation was made by Konstanz-based biologists in their latest study. The researchers see this as an example of nature efficiently copin

items.title: 2% minimum tax on richest households could raise £10 billion
items.summary: Introducing a 2% minimum annual tax on households with more than £100 million in wealth could raise around £10 billion in 2026, while affecting fewer than 1,000 households in the UK, suggests a report by academics at King's College London, the Paris School of Economics and the University of Cal

items.title: Kindness emerges intuitively in young children before reflection catches up, study finds
items.summary: While humans are inherently social beings, psychology studies suggest that their social skills are gradually fine-tuned over time and with experience. Understanding when different social skills emerge and how they typically develop could help devise new strategies that encourage people to behave pro

items.title: It's not just business: Why emotions are central to workplace management
items.summary: "It's not personal, it's strictly business." This line, which echoes throughout Francis Ford Coppola's 1972 gangster film "The Godfather," epitomizes the classic view of management: Doing business is, above all, about making money, even if it means sacrificing all the personal and soc

items.title: Ancient ferns turned Triassic Europe into a wildfire inferno
items.summary: Ancient fern savannahs may have transformed parts of Europe into a wildfire-prone inferno during the end-Triassic mass extinction. New fossil evidence suggests the ferns repeatedly regrew after fires, creating fresh fuel that helped prolong the devastation.

items.title: Hydra rebuilds the body by restoring its internal cellular map
items.summary: Before a hydra can regrow a head or foot, its cells appear to rebuild the internal map that tells them where they are and what they should become next, FMI researchers report. The work suggests that cells need to interpret not only where they are in the body, but also what stage of regeneration they

items.title: Floating colonies: Engineering 3D microbial architectures in liquid cultures
items.summary: Microbes in the gut, lungs and biofilms do not simply exist as perfectly mixed populations; instead, they grow in layered and clustered three-dimensional (3D) arrangements. This spatial organization shapes how nutrients and chemical signals move between neighboring cells and how much oxygen differen

items.title: Colorectal cancer warning signs younger adults should never ignore
items.summary: Colorectal cancer is now the leading cause of cancer death among people under 50, and cases in younger adults continue to rise. Warning signs can include persistent bowel changes, recurring rectal bleeding, dark or narrow stools, unexplained weight loss, and fatigue. These symptoms often have harmle

items.title: Reflooding the Aral Sea could prevent massive CO₂ emissions
items.summary: About 90% of the Aral Sea has dried up. The exposed sediments have already released enormous amounts of carbon dioxide (748 megatons) since 1960. However, through restoration, the lake could once again become a carbon sink. This is shown by a study by the Centre for Advanced Studies of Blanes (CEAB-

items.title: Researchers expand simulation tool to help design the next generation of photonic and quantum devices
items.summary: Many modern technologies, from optical communications and artificial intelligence (AI) hardware to advanced sensors and medical imaging, depend on photonic and semiconductor devices that precisely control the interaction between light and electrons. Designing these devices, however, remains a major

items.title: Lab-grown mini brains may predict which Alzheimer’s treatments will work
items.summary: Miniature brain models grown from patients’ cells revealed striking differences in how Alzheimer’s-related tissue responds to an antidepressant. The organoids and the particles they release could eventually guide more personalized treatments and provide new clues for diagnosing the disease.

items.title: Early respiratory infections may lower higher education completion and adult income
items.summary: Infants exposed to respiratory illnesses through older siblings typically fare worse in education, income and health in adulthood, according to new research from the University of Copenhagen.

items.title: Children become 'climate detectives' to help cool overheating schools
items.summary: Elementary school children equipped with thermal cameras are helping redesign their schools to cope with hotter summers, thanks to a University of Surrey program that gives pupils the tools to spot dangerous heat hotspots and develop their own cooling solutions.

items.title: A strange quantum effect dramatically boosts energy transfer
items.summary: Researchers discovered a proton-assisted mechanism that greatly improves how triplet energy moves between quantum dots and nearby molecules. The proton briefly shifts position, helps coordinate electron movement, and then returns to where it started. This quantum-driven shuttle could offer a powerfu

items.title: In Kyushu's decorated tombs, humans were never meant to be individuals
items.summary: When you first step into Japan's Kyushu tombs, the first thing you notice is nothing at all. "When you enter, you're engulfed by darkness; you can smell earth and stone, and you have the sensation of being enclosed …," said Claudia Zancan, a Ph.D. candidate who examined these sixth to seve

items.title: Can hedging make you a better communicator?
items.summary: "People hedge all the time," says Wharton marketing professor Jonah Berger. "Whether we are talking to a boss or a colleague, a salesperson talking to a client, a marketer trying to pitch something or even talking to family members, we often say things like 'That could work' or 'This

items.title: Why a tiny social media post has mathematicians rethinking AI
items.summary: As millions of people were coming down from the excitement of the FIFA World Cup final at the start of this week, a different kind of excitement was building within the mathematical community. Levent Alpöge, a mathematician who works at the artificial intelligence (AI) company Anthropic, casually an

items.title: Scientists turn radio telescopes into space scanners—sharpening view of hidden orbital threats
items.summary: Scientists have successfully demonstrated a breakthrough in how sensitive radio telescopes can be used to significantly improve the world's ability to detect, track and characterize satellites and space debris in orbit.

items.title: New review highlights gaps in care for First Nations children in Canada
items.summary: A new study finds that First Nations children still face long-standing inequalities in health care access in Canada despite improvements made through the application of Jordan's Principle—a policy designed to ensure First Nations children receive the services they need. The work is published in

items.title: Q&A: What happens when producers are responsible for recycling their products and packaging?
items.summary: Who should pay when a product becomes waste—the company that made it or the public? That question sits at the center of extended producer responsibility (EPR), a policy strategy that shifts recycling costs onto manufacturers to reduce waste and promote more eco-friendly design. Used in more than 100

items.title: New multiplexing scheme accelerates long-distance quantum communication
items.summary: Quantum networks, systems consisting of multiple connected nodes or devices that can transmit quantum information to one another, have the potential to advance future communications. These networks typically leverage entanglement, a quantum phenomenon that prompts two or more distant particles to be

items.title: MIT’s new lidar chip could give self-driving cars a wider view
items.summary: MIT engineers have found a way to give chip-based lidar a wider, clearer view without relying on moving parts. Their design uses differently shaped antennas that can sit close together without scrambling one another’s signals. In tests, the system sharply reduced interference while steering a single

items.title: Reversing soil degradation by 10% could feed 70 million more, global analysis finds
items.summary: Factors such as erosion, dehydration and soil compaction are jeopardizing food supplies for broad swaths of the world's population, according to a study by the University of Bonn, the University of Minnesota, the University of Basel and Hamburg University of Technology. If human-made soil degra

items.title: Nearly 3,000-year-old human remains unearthed in El Salvador
items.summary: Archaeologists in El Salvador exhumed human remains believed to be nearly 3,000 years old Tuesday, the Ministry of Culture said.

items.title: Danish fish fossil cliffs set to gain UNESCO status
items.summary: Two Danish coastal cliffs where paleontologists have uncovered 55-million-year-old fossils of prehistoric wildlife are likely to gain World Heritage status as UNESCO's committee meets in South Korea.

items.title: Private mission launches to extend life of out-of-gas communication satellites
items.summary: A two-armed space robot blasted off on a private salvage operation Tuesday to slap life-extending jetpacks on old satellites running low on fuel thousands of miles up.

items.title: Japan cities 'cruelly hot' for first time
items.summary: Japanese cities are experiencing "cruelly hot days," a new meteorological term devised this year to warn people of intense heat above 40°C (104°F), with heatstroke alerts issued across nearly the entire country.

items.title: 2,000 volunteers join a painstaking effort to restore massive skeleton of prehistoric predator
items.summary: Volunteers armed with chisels, brushes and protective goggles chip away at rock encasing the bones of a massive prehistoric amphibian in a glass-walled laboratory on the ground floor of Warsaw's Copernicus Science Centre.

items.title: Scientists revive a powerful antibiotic that superbugs had defeated
items.summary: Scientists have found a way to revive vancomycin, a powerful antibiotic that some dangerous bacteria have learned to resist. Instead of creating an entirely new drug, researchers paired vancomycin with a small molecule called pghi-4, which blocks a bacterial enzyme linked to resistance. The combinat

items.title: Analysis suggests debt may increase suicide risk
items.summary: In a study published in Economic Inquiry that analyzed debt and income across U.S. counties at the onset of the Great Recession in 2008, investigators uncovered evidence that debt may affect the likelihood of suicide.

items.title: Factors that affect the spininess of plants in different locations
items.summary: Plants often grow spines, thorns and prickles to defend against animals that feed on them, but why this happens in some places more than others has remained a mystery. Research in New Phytologist shows that plant spininess is shaped by browsing mammals, both past and present, together with environme

items.title: Advance could enable enzymatic reactions for chemical manufacturing beyond the boiling point of water
items.summary: Enzymes are proteins that can be harnessed for chemical manufacturing, but they are typically restricted to mild temperatures because proteins unfold and lose their function under heat. In new research published in Angewandte Chemie Novit, investigators found that a porous organic polymer can be use

items.title: Circulating microRNAs offer insights for detecting and treating cachexia in senior dogs
items.summary: Cachexia—a syndrome characterized by progressive muscle wasting—affects both humans and dogs with chronic diseases such as cancer. A recent study in Molecular Oncology found that lower circulating levels of certain microRNAs—small noncoding RNAs that regulate gene expression—often indicate the prese

items.title: Amerika nije imala ovoliko slučajeva ospica u 35 godina
items.summary: BROJ slučajeva ospica u SAD-u dosegnuo je 35-godišnji maksimum, premašivši brojke iz 2025. Stručnjaci za to krive pad procijepljenosti, dezinformacije i kolektivni zaborav opasnosti bolesti.

items.title: Magic mushrooms may reshape the brain long after the trip ends
items.summary: A single dose of psilocybin produced measurable changes in brain activity and possible changes in brain structure that lasted for up to a month. Participants who experienced the greatest increase in flexible, varied brain activity also reported more personal insight and later improvements in well-be

items.title: Australian cave discovery shows grass was burned for magic, cures and curses for 25,000 years
items.summary: The GunaiKurnai, an Australian Aboriginal nation, are the acknowledged traditional custodians of Gippsland and the southern Victorian Alps. Within their territory are caves known from 19th-century ethnographic reports and oral traditions to have been settings for magic, healing and cursing rituals b

items.title: A Week of Smoky Skies Across North America
items.summary: Brown carbon, emitted by wildland fires and tracked over the span of a week in July 2026, worsened air quality in parts of the U.S. and Canada.

items.title: Droughts can boost inclusive institutions, social unity
items.summary: Droughts have an immediate negative impact on agricultural production, but they can also trigger institutional and cultural transformations that help make societies more resilient to environmental crises. This is the main conclusion of the article "Climate shocks, democratization and (a culture of)

items.title: Why low-skilled workers are less likely to participate in continuing education
items.summary: Low-skilled workers are less likely to participate in employer-sponsored continuing education programs than their more highly skilled colleagues. They are less convinced that acquiring additional skills has a positive effect on career success and job satisfaction. This is the finding of a survey con

items.title: New research sheds light on Texas hill country floods
items.summary: Two companion papers in the Bulletin of the American Meteorological Society provide new insight into the causes and predictability of the 2025 Texas Hill Country floods, the deadliest flash flood event in the United States since 1976.

items.title: Rapid bacterial community profiling of equine microbiomes using Oxford Nanopore sequencing
items.summary: Members of the Horse Microbiome Research Group recently published a new study in the Journal of Medical Microbiology, led by Dr. Joy Leng, who is currently undertaking the Alborada Well Foal 2 project. This research aims to understand how the foal gut microbiome develops by investigating the microbi

items.title: Electyrolyzer experiment designated ready-to-fly by NASA
items.summary: A Southwest Research Institute and University of Texas at San Antonio experiment has passed a critical NASA review, designating it ready for parabolic flight testing. The two institutions will evaluate the performance of a patent-pending electrolyzer, the Mars Atmospheric Reactor for Synthesis of Co

items.title: Does dark energy really exist? Our work identifies cracks in the foundations of today's cosmological model
items.summary: According to current understanding, the universe is expanding—and is doing so at an accelerating rate. This is believed to be caused by something called the cosmological constant, which was first proposed by Albert Einstein in his theory of general relativity. In recent decades, it has become better

items.title: Coffee may protect your heart but energy drinks could harm it
items.summary: For most adults, a daily caffeine habit may be safer for the heart than many people assume. A new American Heart Association review found that up to 400 milligrams of caffeine a day, roughly three to five cups of regular black coffee, is generally safe and may even be linked to lower risks of heart

items.title: Leveraging social expectation for charitable donations
items.summary: Avoiding guilt is a powerful motivator for charitable giving, according to a study in PNAS Nexus. Masahiko Haruno and colleagues ran two experiments to determine the most effective psychological framing to encourage donations to WaterAid, an NGO working to provide clean water and sanitation worldwid

items.title: Birds flying in 'V' formation save energy with flatter flaps, new research shows
items.summary: Scientists have known for years that birds like geese and ibises gain an aerodynamic advantage from flying in a "V" formation. Now, researchers from Brown University have provided new insights into the nature of that advantage.

items.title: Urgent cuts to global greenhouse gas emissions needed to avoid worst sea-level rise flooding in the UK, study shows
items.summary: A pioneering study has revealed that by the turn of the next century, at least half a million more people in the UK will be at risk of coastal flooding—a 25% increase compared with now. The research, led by the Met Office and the University of Bristol, quantifies for the first time the scale of the

items.title: Breastfeeding rather than bottle-feeding is seen as the ideal of a good mother in Instagram comments
items.summary: A study by Pompeu Fabra University (UPF) analyzes Instagram discourse that associates the ideal of being a good mother with exclusive breastfeeding and fosters guilt among women who cannot or do not wish to breastfeed, often because of the difficulty of balancing it with other daily responsibilities

items.title: NISAR’s L-Band Radar Reveals ‘Hummingbird’ in Antarctica
items.summary: Description Data from the Earth-orbiting U.S.-India NISAR (NASA-ISRO Synthetic Aperture Radar) satellite’s L-band radar was used to produce an image of Nunatak Zaterjavshijsja — a mountaintop in East Antarctica — poking out amid a stream of ice flowing northeast to the ocean. The obstruction causes

items.title: Testing innovation speeds up detection of deadly tick-borne disease
items.summary: A new method at the University of Missouri could transform how Rocky Mountain spotted fever is diagnosed. The method detects the deadly tick-borne disease in just 40 minutes—a leap that could help patients get lifesaving treatment sooner. The study, "A rapid isothermal RPA–CRISPR/Cas12a assay for de

items.title: UN report: Global hunger levels ease for third consecutive year as regional disparities persist
items.summary: Global hunger declined for a third consecutive year in 2025, demonstrating that progress is possible. However, the improvements remain fragile, unevenly distributed and insufficient to achieve the Sustainable Development Goals by 2030, according to The State of Food Security and Nutrition in the Wor

items.title: Not so terrible, after all: Two‑year‑olds just want to be independent
items.summary: A 2-year-old is lying on the floor yelling, with their socks pulled off after spending 15 minutes trying to get them on in the first place. A caregiver might take a deep breath and think, "Here we go again—another 'terrible twos' tantrum."

items.title: Ruthenium nanoparticles convert captured perchlorate into harmless chloride in water treatment waste
items.summary: For decades, water utilities have relied on giant tanks filled with ion-exchange resin beads to remove perchlorate, a harmful industrial pollutant, from drinking water. The system works by attracting negatively charged perchlorate ions to positively charged resin beads, allowing purified water to fl

items.title: Research explains psychology of helping others feel better about personal disappointments
items.summary: Personal and professional conversations about triumph and disappointment happen every day, but what is the most appropriate way to react when others share their news with us? New research by Bayes Business School (City St George's, University of London) reveals that a common reaction is to mirr

items.title: Gig platforms can simply 'wait out' workers to slash wages, research reveals
items.summary: Researchers from the Max Planck Institute for Intelligent Systems (MPI-IS), the Tübingen AI Center and Ellis Institute Tübingen have conducted a study that uncovers a mathematical strategy explaining how digital labor platforms can systematically suppress wages. The research, titled "Stochastic Wage

items.title: New study into humans' closest ape relatives suggests physical touch can promote social harmony
items.summary: Physical touch may be an important evolutionary tool to help manage social tension and keep the peace. That is according to a new study led by Durham University in the UK, analyzing behaviors of our closest ape relatives, bonobos and chimpanzees.

items.title: Mysterious gas 'bullets' race from Milky Way's only known helium nova at up to 20 million mph
items.summary: Mysterious high-speed "bullets"—clumps of possibly oxygen-rich gas traveling at up to 20 million miles per hour—have been discovered shooting out of the rarest stellar explosion in our galaxy.

items.title: Legalization of sports betting has led to households saving significantly less money
items.summary: As legalized sports betting becomes increasingly accessible through mobile apps such as DraftKings and FanDuel, a new study by BYU finance professors finds that people are saving less to fuel gambling habits.

items.title: Satellite imagery captures the impact of large U.S. dams on river temperatures over the last decade
items.summary: A team of Virginia Tech geoscientists has found a new way to examine the widespread effects of dams on river temperatures: using Landsat thermal infrared imagery to gather data on rivers across the United States. By using satellite imagery instead of site-specific, on-the-ground measurements, resear

items.title: NASA to Host Media Briefing on Roman Telescope, Launching Next Month
items.summary: Media are invited to join NASA for a virtual news conference at 2 p.m. EDT, Wednesday, July 29, to preview the Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope mission, scheduled to launch from the agency’s Kennedy Space Center in Florida on Sunday, Aug. 30.
